g.data-narration: Welcome to Module 4 where we learn essential operations on Binary Search Trees: Insert, Search, Find Min, Max and Count.
g.text: Insertion • Searching • Find Min/Max • Counting Nodes

g.data-narration: These four operations form the backbone of nearly all data structure interactions. Every app you use relies on these fundamental algorithms.
g.text: Core building blocks for algorithms like
• Insert: Add while maintaining BST property

g.data-narration: Before diving into operations, let's understand the key BST property: left subtree has smaller values, right subtree has larger values.
g.text: For any node with value N:

g.data-narration: Insertion algorithm: Start at root, compare keys, go left if smaller or right if larger, insert at first NULL position found.
g.text: 2. Compare new key with current node
3. If smaller → go LEFT; If greater → go RIGHT
4. Insert at first NULL child position

g.data-narration: Here's the recursive implementation. Notice how similar it is to search - we traverse to find the correct position then insert.
Search operation: Similar to insertion but we don't modify the tree. We compare and traverse left or right until we find the target or reach NULL.
The search function returns a pointer to the found node or NULL. Notice how elegant the recursive implementation is.
g.text: if (root == NULL || root->data == key) return root;
Same complexity: O(h) time, O(h) space

g.data-narration: Finding minimum is simple: start at root, keep going LEFT until you reach a node with no left child. That node holds the minimum.
g.text: Rule: Traverse LEFT until you reach a node with no left child

g.data-narration: Finding maximum is the mirror of minimum: start at root, keep going RIGHT until you reach a node with no right child.
g.text: Rule: Traverse RIGHT until you reach a node with no right child
Can also use recursion: O(h) time

g.data-narration: Counting nodes uses recursion: count is 1 (current node) plus count of left subtree plus count of right subtree. Base case: empty tree returns 0.
Time complexity depends on tree height h. For balanced trees, h = log n giving O(log n) performance. For skewed trees, it degrades to O(n).
These operations power real applications: search finds users in databases, min/max tracks stock prices and game scores, count manages shopping carts.
To summarize: Insert adds while preserving BST property, Search finds using comparisons, Min/Max traverse to extremes, Count uses recursive formula 1+left+right.
g.text: • Insert: Compare and go left/right → insert at NULL
• Search: Compare and traverse until found or NULL
• Find Min: Go LEFT until no left child | Find Max: Go RIGHT
• Count: 1 + count(left) + count(right) - recursive tree traversal
